subsystem: Exiv2 image-metadata library: Image::printIFDStructure(), the recursive walker that parses and pretty-prints the TIFF/EXIF Image File Directory (IFD) structure of an image file. It seeks through a BasicIo stream, reads 2-byte directory-entry counts and 12-byte IFD entries, decodes tag/type/count/offset fields with byte-swap helpers, follows pointer offsets to read out-of-line values, and recurses into sub-IFDs (Exif, SubIFDs, MakerNote, IPTC). It processes fully attacker-controlled image files.
provenance: Identified from the fully-qualified method name Image::printIFDStructure and the Exiv2-specific types/enums: Exiv2::PrintStructureOption, kpsBasic/kpsRecursive, DataBuf, BasicIo, byteSwap2/byteSwap4, Error(kerCorruptedMetadata/kerTiffDirectoryTooLarge/kerInvalidTypeValue/kerInvalidMalloc), Internal::indent/stringFormat, IptcData::printStructure, Safe::add, makeSlice/makeSliceUntil, tiffIfd, and the TIFF tag constants 0x8769 (ExifTag), 0x014a (SubIFDs), 0x83bb (IPTCNAA), 0x927c (MakerNote). The sample header 'primevul_train_2877_fixed' and file name cwe835.cpp indicate this is the patched variant of the well-known Exiv2 printIFDStructure image parser.
trust_boundary: The entire input is untrusted: every byte read via io.seek()/io.read() comes from an image file supplied by an attacker (uploaded, emailed, embedded in a document, or crawled). All of the following are hostile and fully attacker-chosen: the 16-bit dirLength (line 19), the per-entry tag/type/count/offset fields (lines 39-42), the pointer offset that out-of-line values live at (lines 42, 82, 124, 128, 135, 149), and the 32-bit next-IFD pointer that continues the do-loop (line 179). The only server-side trust is that BasicIo bounds each individual read to the file; it does NOT bound recursion depth, loop iteration count, or offset cycles.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function signature. `io` is the untrusted image stream; `out` is the text sink for the human-readable dump; `option` selects the verbosity (kpsBasic/kpsRecursive/etc.); `start` is the absolute file offset of the IFD to parse and is attacker-controlled on every recursive call; `bSwap` selects endianness; `c` is a cosmetic character used only in header formatting; `depth` is the current recursion/indent level. Line 4 increments `depth` on entry (and line 187 decrements it on exit) so nested IFDs print indented. Critically, `depth` is used ONLY for indentation — it is never compared against any maximum, so it provides no recursion limit. `bFirst` gates one-time header lines within the directory loop.

A fixed 32-byte scratch buffer `dir` is allocated to hold one directory header (2 bytes) or one directory entry (12 bytes) at a time. 32 comfortably exceeds the 12-byte entry, so reads into `dir` are structurally safe. `bPrint` records whether this invocation should emit output at all; recursion still occurs even when printing is off, because the recursion is driven by `option == kpsRecursive`, not by `bPrint`.

The outer do/while begins. It seeks to `start` and reads 2 bytes into `dir`, which are byte-swapped into `dirLength` — the number of entries in this IFD, a 16-bit value fully controlled by the file (max 65535). If the seek fails or nothing is read, it throws. This is the top of the directory: `dirLength` decides how many 12-byte entries the inner loop will process.

`dirLength` is capped at 500; a larger directory throws kerTiffDirectoryTooLarge. This bounds the inner loop to at most 500 iterations per IFD, which is a real mitigation against a single oversized directory. On the first pass, when printing is enabled, it emits the file-level header line. Note the cap applies per-IFD only — it does nothing to bound how many IFDs are chained (do-while) or how deep recursion goes.

The inner loop iterates once per directory entry. Each entry is 12 bytes read into `dir` (safe: 12 < 32). The 12 bytes are decoded into the standard TIFF IFD-entry fields: `tag` (2 bytes), `type` (2 bytes), `count` (4 bytes, number of components), and `offset` (4 bytes — either an inline value or a file pointer). `count` and `offset` are 32-bit and fully attacker-controlled; they drive every subsequent allocation, copy length, loop bound, and recursion target.

The entry `type` is validated; an unrecognized type logs to stderr and throws kerInvalidTypeValue. This gate ensures the later size-by-type logic (lines 61-66) sees a known type, avoiding an undefined element size. `start = 0` is set but immediately followed by a throw, so it is dead here — the throw unwinds the loop regardless.

what_the_code_does.code:
std::string sp  = "" ; // output spacer

                //prepare to print the value
                uint32_t kount  = isPrintXMP(tag,option) ? count // haul in all the data
                                : isPrintICC(tag,option) ? count // ditto
                                : isStringType(type)     ? (count > 32 ? 32 : count) // restrict long arrays
                                : count > 5              ? 5
                                : count
                                ;
                uint32_t pad    = isStringType(type) ? 1 : 0;
                uint32_t size   = isStringType(type) ? 1
                                : is2ByteType(type)  ? 2
                                : is4ByteType(type)  ? 4
                                : is8ByteType(type)  ? 8
                                : 1
                                ;
what_the_code_does.explanation: `kount` is how many components to PRINT: for XMP/ICC tags it keeps the full `count`, for string types it clamps to 32, otherwise to 5 — this limits the display loops but NOT the allocation. `pad` reserves a trailing NUL byte for string types. `size` is the per-component byte width derived from the type (1/2/4/8), defaulting to 1. `size` and `count` together define how many bytes an entry's value occupies. Because `typeValid` passed, `size` is well-defined and at least 1.

This is the patch's central guard. `allocate` is computed in 64-bit (`(long long) size*count`) as the byte size needed for the value plus padding and 20 slack bytes, then rejected if it exceeds the actual file size io.size(). Because a value cannot be larger than the file it lives in, this caps `size*count` and therefore the buffer and any read into it — this is the fix for the historical integer-overflow/over-read. `buf` is allocated at `(long)allocate`, zero-filled, and the inline 4 bytes from the entry (dir offset 8-11, the `offset` field bytes) are pre-copied so short inline values print without a file read.

what_the_code_does.code:
const bool bOffsetIsPointer = count*size > 4;

                if ( bOffsetIsPointer ) {         // read into buffer
                    size_t   restore = io.tell();  // save
                    io.seek(offset,BasicIo::beg);  // position
                    io.read(buf.pData_,count*size);// read
                    io.seek(restore,BasicIo::beg); // restore
                }
what_the_code_does.explanation: Per the TIFF spec, if the value is larger than 4 bytes it does not fit inline and `offset` is a file pointer. `bOffsetIsPointer` tests `count*size > 4` — but note this multiplication is done in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic here, unlike the 64-bit computation on line 71. When it is a pointer, the current position is saved, the code seeks to the attacker's `offset`, reads `count*size` bytes into `buf`, and restores. The read length is again `count*size` in 32-bit.

The printing block. It formats the entry header (address, tag name via .c_str() on a temporary std::string from tagName(tag), type name, count, offset). It then dumps the value according to type: short types byteSwap2 each of `kount` 2-byte words, long types byteSwap4 each 4-byte word, rational types two 32-bit words per component (k*size+0 and k*size+4), and string types via binaryToString over a slice of length `kount`. Every index into `buf` is `k*size` with k < kount <= count, and `buf` holds at least size*count+20 bytes, so these reads stay inside the allocation. A ' ...' suffix marks truncated (kount<count) output.

The recursive descent for the Exif IFD, SubIFDs, or any entry whose type is tiffIfd. For each of `count` sub-pointers stored in `buf`, it decodes a new `offset` (attacker-controlled) and calls printIFDStructure on it, then restores position. This is the core recursion: the loop bound `count` and the recursion target `offset` are BOTH from the file, and there is no depth/visited guard on the recursive call — `depth` is passed through unchanged and merely incremented on entry.

The IPTC branch. It first uses Safe::add(count, offset) to detect wraparound and rejects the entry if count+offset exceeds the file size — an overflow-safe bound that is a deliberate hardening. It then allocates a std::vector<byte> of `count` bytes (RAII, so no leak), reads up to `count` bytes from `offset`, and hands the actual read length to IptcData::printStructure. Because count+offset is bounded by io.size(), `count` itself is bounded by the file size, so the vector allocation is bounded. This branch is well-guarded.

The MakerNote branch, taken only when count>10. It reads 10 bytes into a 20-byte stack buffer `bytes`, NUL-terminates at index 10 (bytes[10]=0, safely within [0,19]), and compares to "Nikon". For a Nikon note it heap-allocates `count-jump` bytes (count>10 guarantees this is positive), reads that many bytes, wraps them in a MemIo, and parses as an embedded TIFF; the buffer is released with matching delete[] on line 158 (the new[]/delete[] pairing is correct). Otherwise it treats `offset` as another IFD and recurses via printIFDStructure — a second unbounded recursion site.

For XMP tags it NUL-terminates `buf` at index `count` and prints it as a C string; for ICC it writes exactly `count` bytes. For XMP/ICC, kount==count and size>=1, so `allocate = size*count + pad + 20 >= count + 20`, meaning index `count` and a `count`-byte write both stay within `buf`. The trailing +20/pad slack in the allocation is what makes the `buf.pData_[count]=0` write in-bounds.

After processing the directory, if `start` was non-zero it reads the 4-byte next-IFD pointer and sets `start` to it (or 0 if this directory was tooBig). The do/while repeats as long as `start` is non-zero, forming the standard chained-IFD walk. Because the next `start` is read straight from the file with no bound and no record of previously-visited offsets, the file controls whether and where the loop continues. Finally the END marker is printed, the stream flushed, and `depth` decremented to mirror the entry increment.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Uncontrolled recursion / stack exhaustion (the CWE-835 the file is named for, more precisely CWE-674). When option==kpsRecursive, an Exif/SubIFD/tiffIfd entry recurses into printIFDStructure at an attacker-chosen `offset`. `depth` is incremented on entry (line 4) but is NEVER compared to any limit — it is used only for indentation. An attacker crafts an IFD whose sub-IFD pointer references another IFD (or points back at itself/an ancestor) so each recursion reads a valid-looking directory that again contains a sub-IFD pointer. There is no visited-offset set and no depth cap, so the call stack grows until it is exhausted, crashing the process (denial of service) or, in some builds, enabling stack-clash effects. The second recursion site at line 162 (MakerNote non-Nikon branch) has the identical defect. The invariant the implementation must uphold: bound recursion by a hard depth limit (e.g. refuse when depth exceeds a small constant) and/or track already-visited IFD offsets to break cycles.

Loop with attacker-controlled continuation (CWE-835 infinite loop). The do/while continues while `start` is non-zero, and the next `start` is read verbatim from the file's next-IFD pointer with no check that it advances, differs from a previously-visited offset, or points anywhere sane. An attacker builds a directory at offset A whose next-IFD pointer is B, and a directory at B whose next-IFD pointer is A (each kept under the 500-entry cap so `tooBig` never fires). The loop then ping-pongs between the two directories forever, re-reading and re-emitting output without termination — a CPU/output denial of service that does not even require recursion. The per-IFD `dirLength <= 500` cap (line 21) does nothing to stop this because it bounds a single directory, not the chain length. Safe design: maintain a set of visited IFD offsets and reject a `start` that has already been processed (or require strictly increasing offsets).

what_could_go_wrong.code:
const bool bOffsetIsPointer = count*size > 4;

                if ( bOffsetIsPointer ) {         // read into buffer
                    size_t   restore = io.tell();  // save
                    io.seek(offset,BasicIo::beg);  // position
                    io.read(buf.pData_,count*size);// read
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: 32-bit unsigned multiply inconsistency (CWE-190, latent). The allocation guard on line 71 computes the value size in 64-bit as `(long long) size*count`, but the pointer test on line 78 and the read length on line 83 use `count*size` evaluated in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic. If `count*size` overflows 2^32, the 64-bit `allocate` on line 71 becomes huge and is rejected by the io.size() check (line 72), so the dangerous path is defended — but the two expressions can DISAGREE about the value size. This is the class of bug that historically produced an out-of-bounds read in byteSwap4 (indices k*size at lines 98/103) before the allocate guard was added; the guard mitigates it, so residual risk is limited to reading zero-filled (memset) slack within `buf` rather than a true over-read. The invariant: use a single overflow-checked width computation (the 64-bit one) consistently for the pointer test, the read length, and the allocation, so buffer size and read length can never diverge.

One-past-write into `buf` at index `count` (CWE-787 class, currently in-bounds). This writes at `buf.pData_[count]` and then prints the buffer as a NUL-terminated C string. It is safe ONLY because for XMP tags size>=1 and pad guarantee `allocate = size*count + pad + 20` exceeds `count`, and the whole buffer was memset to zero. The safety therefore depends entirely on the +20 slack and on `size` never being 0 (guaranteed by typeValid + the size default of 1). The invariant to keep this correct: any refactor must preserve that the allocation strictly exceeds `count` for XMP/ICC tags; if the +20 slack or the size>=1 guarantee were ever removed, `buf.pData_[count]=0` becomes a one-byte heap overflow and the subsequent unbounded C-string print (line 171) could over-read if the terminator is absent.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
byte* bytes=new byte[count-jump] ;  // allocate memory
                            io.read(bytes,count-jump)        ;  // read
                            MemIo memIo(bytes,count-jump)    ;  // create a file
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Raw new[] with an exception-exposed lifetime (CWE-401/CWE-762 class, currently benign). `count-jump` is safe from underflow because the branch requires count>10 and jump==10, so the subtraction is at least 1. The new[] is correctly released by delete[] on line 158, so under normal flow there is no leak or mismatch. The residual concern is exception safety: printTiffStructure(memIo,...) on line 157 can throw (it recurses into the same untrusted-parsing machinery), and because `bytes` is a raw pointer rather than std::vector/std::unique_ptr, an exception between new[] and delete[] leaks the buffer. The invariant: own heap buffers with RAII (std::vector<byte> as the IPTC branch already does on line 136) so the delete happens on every path including exceptional unwinding.

summary: The dominant risk is denial of service from attacker-controlled traversal: printIFDStructure recurses into sub-IFDs at file-supplied offsets with no depth cap or cycle detection (unbounded recursion / stack exhaustion), and its outer do/while follows file-supplied next-IFD pointers with no visited-offset guard (potential infinite loop). Both are reachable from any image parsed with kpsRecursive. The patched allocation guard (64-bit size check against io.size) and the Safe::add IPTC check defuse the historical integer-overflow heap over-reads, leaving those as latent/mitigated rather than live; memory management (new[]/delete[], std::vector) is otherwise correctly paired.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: printIFDStructure(...) signature, uint32_t start / int depth params (line 2)
reviewed_anchors.reason: start and depth are the recursion/loop drivers; depth is never bounded
increments recursion level but is compared to no limit; used only for indentation
fixed 32-byte buffer; all reads into it are 2/12/4 bytes, well within bounds
16-bit attacker value but capped at 500 on line 22, bounding the inner loop
effective per-IFD cap; a real mitigation, not a defect

reviewed_anchors.anchor: for ( int i = 0 ; i < dirLength ; i++ ) (line 30)
reviewed_anchors.reason: bounded by dirLength<=500
12 bytes into a 32-byte buffer, in-bounds
count and offset are 32-bit attacker-controlled and drive allocations, reads, loops, and recursion
validates type so size-by-type is well-defined; defensive
bounds display loops; mitigation
well-defined element width, minimum 1, after typeValid

reviewed_anchors.anchor: long long allocate = (long long) size*count + pad+20; guard vs io.size() (lines 71-74)
reviewed_anchors.reason: 64-bit computation + file-size check bounds buffer and reads; this is the patch
(long) cast; allocate already bounded by io.size(), so no meaningful truncation on LP64
4 bytes from dir offset 8 (<=12) into buf sized >=size*count+20; in-bounds
32-bit multiply here vs 64-bit on line 71 — inconsistent width; latent CWE-190, mitigated by allocate guard
k<kount<=count and buf>=size*count+20, so indices stay in bounds
unbounded recursion at attacker offset, no depth/cycle guard
overflow-safe bound; hardening, and bounds the vector(count) allocation
RAII allocation bounded by the Safe::add/io.size() check; no leak
reads 10 and writes index 10 into a 20-byte buffer; strcmp is NUL-terminated; in-bounds
count-jump cannot underflow (count>10) and pairing is correct, but raw new[] leaks if printTiffStructure throws
second unbounded-recursion site at attacker offset (same class as lines 121-126)
one-past write and unbounded C-string print; in-bounds only due to +20 slack and size>=1
count bytes from buf sized >=count; may print zero-filled slack but no over-read
next-IFD pointer from file with no visited-set; cyclic chain loops forever (CWE-835)

findings.title: Unbounded recursion into sub-IFDs at attacker-controlled offset (stack exhaustion)
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled recursion / stack exhaustion (denial of service)
findings.invariant: Recursion depth must be hard-capped (reject when depth exceeds a small constant) and/or IFD offsets already visited on the current chain must be tracked so cyclic/self-referential sub-IFD pointers cannot recurse indefinitely.
findings.failure_mode: With option==kpsRecursive, a crafted image whose Exif/SubIFD/tiffIfd entry points to another IFD (or back at itself/an ancestor) drives printIFDStructure to call itself with a fresh attacker-chosen offset on every level. depth is incremented on entry but never checked, so the call stack grows without limit until it is exhausted and the process crashes — a remote denial of service triggered simply by parsing the file. The MakerNote non-Nikon branch (line 162) is a second instance of the same recursion with no additional guard.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no caller-side or global depth limit exists and that printIFDStructure keeps no visited-offset set; verify that a self-referential SubIFD/Exif pointer actually re-enters the function unbounded.

findings.title: Infinite loop over chained next-IFD pointers (no cycle detection)
findings.bug_class: Loop with attacker-controlled, unbounded exit condition (denial of service)
findings.invariant: The next-IFD offset must be validated against previously-visited offsets (a visited set, or a requirement that offsets strictly increase) so a chain cannot cycle; the loop must be guaranteed to terminate.
findings.failure_mode: The do/while continues while start is non-zero, and the next start is the file's next-IFD pointer read verbatim (line 179). An attacker links directory A's next pointer to B and B's next pointer to A, each kept at <=500 entries so tooBig never fires, causing the loop to ping-pong between the two directories forever — unbounded CPU consumption and output, without any recursion. The per-IFD dirLength<=500 cap does not help because it bounds one directory, not the chain.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the implementation keeps no record of visited IFD offsets and does not require monotonically increasing start values; build a two-node offset cycle and verify the loop does not terminate.

findings.title: Inconsistent 32-bit vs 64-bit value-size arithmetic (latent integer-overflow over-read)
findings.code:
const bool bOffsetIsPointer = count*size > 4;

                if ( bOffsetIsPointer ) {         // read into buffer
                    size_t   restore = io.tell();  // save
                    io.seek(offset,BasicIo::beg);  // position
                    io.read(buf.pData_,count*size);// read
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow in size arithmetic (out-of-bounds read primitive)
findings.invariant: The value width must be computed once with overflow checking (the 64-bit size*count) and that single value used consistently for the pointer test, the read length, and the buffer allocation, so read length can never exceed allocated size.
findings.failure_mode: Line 71 sizes the buffer with a 64-bit `(long long) size*count`, but lines 78 and 83 recompute `count*size` in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic. If the true product exceeds 2^32 the 64-bit allocate is huge and rejected by the io.size() guard, so the live over-read path (historically an out-of-bounds read in byteSwap4 at lines 98/103) is mitigated by the patch. Residual risk: the two widths can disagree, and any weakening of the allocate guard would re-expose a read length larger than the buffer. This is the integer-overflow-to-heap-over-read class the surrounding guard was added to fix.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify the allocate guard on line 72 is reached on every path that performs the count*size read, and that count*size cannot overflow 32-bit while allocate (64-bit) stays under io.size().

findings.title: Raw new[] buffer leaks if embedded-TIFF parse throws
findings.anchor: byte* bytes=new byte[count-jump]; ... printTiffStructure(memIo,...); delete[] bytes
findings.code:
byte* bytes=new byte[count-jump] ;  // allocate memory
                            io.read(bytes,count-jump)        ;  // read
                            MemIo memIo(bytes,count-jump)    ;  // create a file
                            printTiffStructure(memIo,out,option,depth);
                            delete[] bytes                   ;  // free
findings.bug_class: Memory leak on exception path (non-RAII ownership)
findings.invariant: Heap buffers holding parsed untrusted data must be owned by RAII (std::vector<byte> or std::unique_ptr<byte[]>) so the deallocation runs on every path, including stack unwinding from a thrown Error.
findings.failure_mode: `bytes` is a raw new[] pointer freed only by the delete[] on line 158. printTiffStructure on line 157 recurses into the same untrusted-parsing code and can throw (e.g. kerCorruptedMetadata/kerInvalidTypeValue); the throw unwinds past line 158, leaking the buffer. Repeated over many crafted MakerNote entries this is an attacker-driven memory leak. count-jump itself cannot underflow because the branch guard requires count>10 with jump==10.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm printTiffStructure can throw and that no surrounding try/catch frees `bytes`; check whether the buffer should be a std::vector like the IPTC branch on line 136.

findings.title: One-past NUL write and unbounded C-string print for XMP tags
findings.bug_class: Potential out-of-bounds write / over-read (bounds depend on allocation slack)
findings.invariant: For any tag that writes buf.pData_[count], the allocation must strictly exceed count bytes (guaranteed today by size>=1 and the +pad+20 slack in `allocate`), and the buffer must be NUL-terminated before being printed as a C string.
findings.failure_mode: For XMP tags the code writes buf.pData_[count]=0 and prints buf as a NUL-terminated string. This is in-bounds only because `allocate = size*count + pad + 20` exceeds count (size>=1) and the whole buffer was memset to zero. The correctness is entirely dependent on that +20 slack and size>=1; if a refactor tightened the allocation or allowed size==0, this becomes a one-byte heap overflow, and the C-string print on line 171 could over-read past the value if the terminator were ever absent.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify that for every tag reaching line 170 the buffer size is strictly greater than count, and that size can never be 0 for such tags.

audit_checklist: Add a hard recursion-depth cap in printIFDStructure and reject when exceeded; confirm both recursion sites (lines 125 and 162) are covered.
Track visited IFD file offsets (or require strictly increasing offsets) to break cycles in BOTH the recursive descent and the do/while next-IFD chain (line 179).
Use a single overflow-checked 64-bit width for the pointer test, the read length, and the allocation so line 78/83 (count*size, 32-bit) cannot diverge from line 71 (size*count, 64-bit).
Confirm the allocate>io.size() guard (line 72) is unavoidably reached before every count*size read into buf.
Replace the raw new byte[count-jump]/delete[] pair (lines 154-158) with std::vector<byte> or unique_ptr so it is exception-safe against a throwing printTiffStructure.
Confirm buf.pData_[count]=0 (line 170) is always strictly inside buf: size>=1 and the +pad+20 slack must hold for every XMP/ICC tag.
Verify Safe::add(count,offset) (line 130) is the model for all offset+length checks and that count-derived allocations elsewhere are similarly bounded by io.size().
Confirm typeValid (line 45) actually rejects every type not handled by the size table so size is never 0.
